# transcript_type: auto-generated
# language: en
[00:00] Welcome to this comprehensive deep dive into transformer architectures and large language models.
[00:05] In this lecture we will cover attention mechanisms, positional encodings, multi-head attention,
[00:10] feed-forward sublayers, layer normalization, residual connections, and the training dynamics
[00:15] that make these models so powerful. We will also discuss practical considerations like tokenization,
[00:20] vocabulary size, context length limitations, and inference optimization techniques such as
[00:25] key-value caching and speculative decoding. By the end of this session you will have a thorough
[00:30] understanding of how modern language models work under the hood and how to apply them effectively.
[00:35] The attention mechanism is the core innovation of the transformer. Unlike recurrent networks
[00:40] that process tokens sequentially, attention allows every token to directly attend to every other
[00:45] token in the sequence. The attention score between two tokens is computed as the dot product of
[00:50] their query and key vectors, scaled by the square root of the dimension, then passed through
[00:55] a softmax to produce a probability distribution. This distribution is used to compute a weighted
[01:00] sum of value vectors. The result is a context-aware representation for each token that captures
[01:05] relationships across the entire sequence regardless of distance.
[01:10] Multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel with different learned projections.
[01:15] Each head can specialize in different types of relationships. Some heads learn syntactic dependencies,
[01:20] others learn semantic associations, and others capture positional patterns. The outputs of all heads
[01:25] are concatenated and projected back to the model dimension. This gives the model much richer
[01:30] representational capacity than a single attention operation could provide.
[01:35] Positional encodings are added to token embeddings to inject information about position. The
[01:40] original transformer used sinusoidal encodings with different frequencies for each dimension.
[01:45] Later work explored learned positional embeddings, relative position encodings, and rotary
[01:50] position embeddings. Rotary encodings have become the dominant approach in modern models
[01:55] because they generalize well to sequence lengths beyond those seen during training.
[02:00] Layer normalization stabilizes training by normalizing the activations within each layer.
[02:05] Pre-norm architectures, where layer norm is applied before the attention and feed-forward
[02:10] sublayers rather than after, have been found to train more stably and are used in most modern
[02:15] large language models. Residual connections allow gradients to flow directly through the
[02:20] network during backpropagation, enabling training of very deep models.
[02:25] The feed-forward sublayer consists of two linear transformations with a non-linearity in between.
[02:30] The inner dimension is typically four times the model dimension. Modern models use the SwiGLU
[02:35] activation function, which is a variant of the gated linear unit, instead of the original ReLU.
[02:40] This change was found to improve performance across a wide range of tasks.
[02:45] Tokenization converts raw text into a sequence of integer indices that the model can process.
[02:50] Byte pair encoding is the most common tokenization algorithm. It starts with individual characters
[02:55] and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build a vocabulary of subword units.
[03:00] This balances vocabulary coverage with sequence length. Typical modern models use vocabularies
[03:05] of around thirty thousand to one hundred thousand tokens.
[03:10] Training large language models requires enormous computational resources. Models with billions
[03:15] of parameters are trained on trillions of tokens using hundreds or thousands of GPUs. Mixed
[03:20] precision training uses 16-bit floating point for most computations and 32-bit for weight updates
[03:25] to reduce memory usage and increase throughput. Gradient checkpointing trades compute for
[03:30] memory by recomputing activations during the backward pass instead of storing them.
[03:35] The training objective for language models is next-token prediction. The model is trained to
[03:40] maximize the log probability of the next token given all preceding tokens. This deceptively
[03:45] simple objective, when applied at massive scale, leads to models that acquire broad knowledge
[03:50] and surprising capabilities including reasoning, code generation, and instruction following.
[03:55] Instruction tuning is a fine-tuning procedure that teaches models to follow user instructions.
[04:00] A dataset of instruction-response pairs is collected and the model is fine-tuned on these examples.
[04:05] Reinforcement learning from human feedback further aligns the model by training a reward model
[04:10] on human preference data and using it to guide the policy model toward outputs that humans rate
[04:15] as more helpful, harmless, and honest.
[04:20] Inference optimization is critical for deploying large models efficiently. Key-value caching
[04:25] stores the key and value tensors from the attention mechanism so they do not need to be
[04:30] recomputed for tokens that have already been processed. Speculative decoding uses a small
[04:35] draft model to generate multiple candidate tokens and then verifies them in parallel with the
[04:40] large model, achieving significant speedups for autoregressive generation.
[04:45] Quantization reduces the precision of model weights from 32-bit or 16-bit floating point to
[04:50] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[04:55] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[05:00] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[05:05] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.
[05:10] Context length is a fundamental limitation of transformer models due to the quadratic scaling
[05:15] of attention with sequence length. Techniques like sliding window attention, sparse attention,
[05:20] and linear attention approximate the full attention mechanism at lower computational cost.
[05:25] Flash attention is a hardware-aware implementation that significantly reduces memory usage
[05:30] and speeds up attention computation by tiling the operation to fit in GPU on-chip memory.
[05:35] Retrieval augmented generation combines a language model with a retrieval system. At inference
[05:40] time, relevant documents are retrieved from a large corpus and included in the context window
[05:45] alongside the user query. This allows models to access up-to-date information without retraining
[05:50] and reduces hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved evidence.
[05:55] Evaluation of language models is challenging because the space of possible outputs is vast and
[06:00] quality is often subjective. Automatic benchmarks like MMLU, HellaSwag, and HumanEval measure
[06:05] specific capabilities. Human evaluation through preference comparisons or ratings provides a
[06:10] more holistic assessment. The ELO rating system borrowed from chess has been adapted to rank
[06:15] models based on human pairwise preferences across diverse tasks.
[06:20] Fine-tuning on domain-specific data is an effective way to improve model performance on
[06:25] specialized tasks. Parameter efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA and prefix tuning reduce
[06:30] the cost of adaptation by only training a small number of additional parameters. LoRA adds
[06:35] low-rank decomposition matrices to weight matrices and only trains these additions, leaving
[06:40] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.
[06:45] Welcome to this comprehensive deep dive into transformer architectures and large language models.
[06:50] In this lecture we will cover attention mechanisms, positional encodings, multi-head attention,
[06:55] feed-forward sublayers, layer normalization, residual connections, and the training dynamics
[07:00] that make these models so powerful. We will also discuss practical considerations like tokenization,
[07:05] vocabulary size, context length limitations, and inference optimization techniques such as
[07:10] key-value caching and speculative decoding. By the end of this session you will have a thorough
[07:15] understanding of how modern language models work under the hood and how to apply them effectively.
[07:20] The attention mechanism is the core innovation of the transformer. Unlike recurrent networks
[07:25] that process tokens sequentially, attention allows every token to directly attend to every other
[07:30] token in the sequence. The attention score between two tokens is computed as the dot product of
[07:35] their query and key vectors, scaled by the square root of the dimension, then passed through
[07:40] a softmax to produce a probability distribution. This distribution is used to compute a weighted
[07:45] sum of value vectors. The result is a context-aware representation for each token that captures
[07:50] relationships across the entire sequence regardless of distance.

[08:00] Each head can specialize in different types of relationships. Some heads learn syntactic dependencies,
[08:05] others learn semantic associations, and others capture positional patterns. The outputs of all heads
[08:10] are concatenated and projected back to the model dimension. This gives the model much richer
[08:15] representational capacity than a single attention operation could provide.

[08:35] position embeddings. Rotary encodings have become the dominant approach in modern models
[08:40] because they generalize well to sequence lengths beyond those seen during training.

[09:00] large language models. Residual connections allow gradients to flow directly through the
[09:05] network during backpropagation, enabling training of very deep models.

[09:15] The inner dimension is typically four times the model dimension. Modern models use the SwiGLU
[09:20] activation function, which is a variant of the gated linear unit, instead of the original ReLU.
[09:25] This change was found to improve performance across a wide range of tasks.
[09:30] Tokenization converts raw text into a sequence of integer indices that the model can process.
[09:35] Byte pair encoding is the most common tokenization algorithm. It starts with individual characters
[09:40] and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build a vocabulary of subword units.
[09:45] This balances vocabulary coverage with sequence length. Typical modern models use vocabularies
[09:50] of around thirty thousand to one hundred thousand tokens.
[09:55] Training large language models requires enormous computational resources. Models with billions
[10:00] of parameters are trained on trillions of tokens using hundreds or thousands of GPUs. Mixed
[10:05] precision training uses 16-bit floating point for most computations and 32-bit for weight updates
[10:10] to reduce memory usage and increase throughput. Gradient checkpointing trades compute for
[10:15] memory by recomputing activations during the backward pass instead of storing them.
[10:20] The training objective for language models is next-token prediction. The model is trained to
[10:25] maximize the log probability of the next token given all preceding tokens. This deceptively
[10:30] simple objective, when applied at massive scale, leads to models that acquire broad knowledge
[10:35] and surprising capabilities including reasoning, code generation, and instruction following.

[11:00] as more helpful, harmless, and honest.

[11:35] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[11:40] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[11:45] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[11:50] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.

[12:00] of attention with sequence length. Techniques like sliding window attention, sparse attention,

[19:15] alongside the user query. This allows models to access up-to-date information without retraining
[19:20] and reduces hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved evidence.

[19:30] quality is often subjective. Automatic benchmarks like MMLU, HellaSwag, and HumanEval measure
[19:35] specific capabilities. Human evaluation through preference comparisons or ratings provides a
[19:40] more holistic assessment. The ELO rating system borrowed from chess has been adapted to rank
[19:45] models based on human pairwise preferences across diverse tasks.
[19:50] Fine-tuning on domain-specific data is an effective way to improve model performance on
[19:55] specialized tasks. Parameter efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA and prefix tuning reduce
[20:00] the cost of adaptation by only training a small number of additional parameters. LoRA adds
[20:05] low-rank decomposition matrices to weight matrices and only trains these additions, leaving
[20:10] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.

[20:25] feed-forward sublayers, layer normalization, residual connections, and the training dynamics

[20:35] vocabulary size, context length limitations, and inference optimization techniques such as
[20:40] key-value caching and speculative decoding. By the end of this session you will have a thorough
[20:45] understanding of how modern language models work under the hood and how to apply them effectively.

[20:55] that process tokens sequentially, attention allows every token to directly attend to every other

[27:10] feed-forward sublayers, layer normalization, residual connections, and the training dynamics
[27:15] that make these models so powerful. We will also discuss practical considerations like tokenization,
[27:20] vocabulary size, context length limitations, and inference optimization techniques such as
[27:25] key-value caching and speculative decoding. By the end of this session you will have a thorough
[27:30] understanding of how modern language models work under the hood and how to apply them effectively.

[27:40] that process tokens sequentially, attention allows every token to directly attend to every other

[27:50] their query and key vectors, scaled by the square root of the dimension, then passed through

[28:05] relationships across the entire sequence regardless of distance.

[28:20] others learn semantic associations, and others capture positional patterns. The outputs of all heads
[28:25] are concatenated and projected back to the model dimension. This gives the model much richer

[29:30] The inner dimension is typically four times the model dimension. Modern models use the SwiGLU
[29:35] activation function, which is a variant of the gated linear unit, instead of the original ReLU.
[29:40] This change was found to improve performance across a wide range of tasks.
[29:45] Tokenization converts raw text into a sequence of integer indices that the model can process.
[29:50] Byte pair encoding is the most common tokenization algorithm. It starts with individual characters
[29:55] and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build a vocabulary of subword units.
[30:00] This balances vocabulary coverage with sequence length. Typical modern models use vocabularies
[30:05] of around thirty thousand to one hundred thousand tokens.
[30:10] Training large language models requires enormous computational resources. Models with billions
[30:15] of parameters are trained on trillions of tokens using hundreds or thousands of GPUs. Mixed
[30:20] precision training uses 16-bit floating point for most computations and 32-bit for weight updates
[30:25] to reduce memory usage and increase throughput. Gradient checkpointing trades compute for
[30:30] memory by recomputing activations during the backward pass instead of storing them.
[30:35] The training objective for language models is next-token prediction. The model is trained to
[30:40] maximize the log probability of the next token given all preceding tokens. This deceptively
[30:45] simple objective, when applied at massive scale, leads to models that acquire broad knowledge
[30:50] and surprising capabilities including reasoning, code generation, and instruction following.
[30:55] Instruction tuning is a fine-tuning procedure that teaches models to follow user instructions.
[31:00] A dataset of instruction-response pairs is collected and the model is fine-tuned on these examples.
[31:05] Reinforcement learning from human feedback further aligns the model by training a reward model
[31:10] on human preference data and using it to guide the policy model toward outputs that humans rate
[31:15] as more helpful, harmless, and honest.
[31:20] Inference optimization is critical for deploying large models efficiently. Key-value caching
[31:25] stores the key and value tensors from the attention mechanism so they do not need to be
[31:30] recomputed for tokens that have already been processed. Speculative decoding uses a small
[31:35] draft model to generate multiple candidate tokens and then verifies them in parallel with the
[31:40] large model, achieving significant speedups for autoregressive generation.
[31:45] Quantization reduces the precision of model weights from 32-bit or 16-bit floating point to
[31:50] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[31:55] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[32:00] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[32:05] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.
[32:10] Context length is a fundamental limitation of transformer models due to the quadratic scaling
[32:15] of attention with sequence length. Techniques like sliding window attention, sparse attention,
[32:20] and linear attention approximate the full attention mechanism at lower computational cost.
[32:25] Flash attention is a hardware-aware implementation that significantly reduces memory usage
[32:30] and speeds up attention computation by tiling the operation to fit in GPU on-chip memory.
[32:35] Retrieval augmented generation combines a language model with a retrieval system. At inference
[32:40] time, relevant documents are retrieved from a large corpus and included in the context window
[32:45] alongside the user query. This allows models to access up-to-date information without retraining
[32:50] and reduces hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved evidence.
[32:55] Evaluation of language models is challenging because the space of possible outputs is vast and
[33:00] quality is often subjective. Automatic benchmarks like MMLU, HellaSwag, and HumanEval measure
[33:05] specific capabilities. Human evaluation through preference comparisons or ratings provides a
[33:10] more holistic assessment. The ELO rating system borrowed from chess has been adapted to rank
[33:15] models based on human pairwise preferences across diverse tasks.
[33:20] Fine-tuning on domain-specific data is an effective way to improve model performance on
[33:25] specialized tasks. Parameter efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA and prefix tuning reduce
[33:30] the cost of adaptation by only training a small number of additional parameters. LoRA adds
[33:35] low-rank decomposition matrices to weight matrices and only trains these additions, leaving
[33:40] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.
[33:45] Welcome to this comprehensive deep dive into transformer architectures and large language models.
[33:50] In this lecture we will cover attention mechanisms, positional encodings, multi-head attention,
[33:55] feed-forward sublayers, layer normalization, residual connections, and the training dynamics
[34:00] that make these models so powerful. We will also discuss practical considerations like tokenization,
[34:05] vocabulary size, context length limitations, and inference optimization techniques such as
[34:10] key-value caching and speculative decoding. By the end of this session you will have a thorough
[34:15] understanding of how modern language models work under the hood and how to apply them effectively.
[34:20] The attention mechanism is the core innovation of the transformer. Unlike recurrent networks
[34:25] that process tokens sequentially, attention allows every token to directly attend to every other
[34:30] token in the sequence. The attention score between two tokens is computed as the dot product of
[34:35] their query and key vectors, scaled by the square root of the dimension, then passed through
[34:40] a softmax to produce a probability distribution. This distribution is used to compute a weighted
[34:45] sum of value vectors. The result is a context-aware representation for each token that captures
[34:50] relationships across the entire sequence regardless of distance.
[34:55] Multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel with different learned projections.
[35:00] Each head can specialize in different types of relationships. Some heads learn syntactic dependencies,
[35:05] others learn semantic associations, and others capture positional patterns. The outputs of all heads
[35:10] are concatenated and projected back to the model dimension. This gives the model much richer
[35:15] representational capacity than a single attention operation could provide.
[35:20] Positional encodings are added to token embeddings to inject information about position. The
[35:25] original transformer used sinusoidal encodings with different frequencies for each dimension.
[35:30] Later work explored learned positional embeddings, relative position encodings, and rotary
[35:35] position embeddings. Rotary encodings have become the dominant approach in modern models
[35:40] because they generalize well to sequence lengths beyond those seen during training.
[35:45] Layer normalization stabilizes training by normalizing the activations within each layer.
[35:50] Pre-norm architectures, where layer norm is applied before the attention and feed-forward
[35:55] sublayers rather than after, have been found to train more stably and are used in most modern
[36:00] large language models. Residual connections allow gradients to flow directly through the
[36:05] network during backpropagation, enabling training of very deep models.
[36:10] The feed-forward sublayer consists of two linear transformations with a non-linearity in between.
[36:15] The inner dimension is typically four times the model dimension. Modern models use the SwiGLU
[36:20] activation function, which is a variant of the gated linear unit, instead of the original ReLU.
[36:25] This change was found to improve performance across a wide range of tasks.
[36:30] Tokenization converts raw text into a sequence of integer indices that the model can process.
[36:35] Byte pair encoding is the most common tokenization algorithm. It starts with individual characters
[36:40] and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build a vocabulary of subword units.
[36:45] This balances vocabulary coverage with sequence length. Typical modern models use vocabularies
[36:50] of around thirty thousand to one hundred thousand tokens.
[36:55] Training large language models requires enormous computational resources. Models with billions
[37:00] of parameters are trained on trillions of tokens using hundreds or thousands of GPUs. Mixed
[37:05] precision training uses 16-bit floating point for most computations and 32-bit for weight updates
[37:10] to reduce memory usage and increase throughput. Gradient checkpointing trades compute for
[37:15] memory by recomputing activations during the backward pass instead of storing them.
[37:20] The training objective for language models is next-token prediction. The model is trained to
[37:25] maximize the log probability of the next token given all preceding tokens. This deceptively
[37:30] simple objective, when applied at massive scale, leads to models that acquire broad knowledge
[37:35] and surprising capabilities including reasoning, code generation, and instruction following.
[37:40] Instruction tuning is a fine-tuning procedure that teaches models to follow user instructions.
[37:45] A dataset of instruction-response pairs is collected and the model is fine-tuned on these examples.
[37:50] Reinforcement learning from human feedback further aligns the model by training a reward model
[37:55] on human preference data and using it to guide the policy model toward outputs that humans rate
[38:00] as more helpful, harmless, and honest.
[38:05] Inference optimization is critical for deploying large models efficiently. Key-value caching
[38:10] stores the key and value tensors from the attention mechanism so they do not need to be
[38:15] recomputed for tokens that have already been processed. Speculative decoding uses a small
[38:20] draft model to generate multiple candidate tokens and then verifies them in parallel with the
[38:25] large model, achieving significant speedups for autoregressive generation.
[38:30] Quantization reduces the precision of model weights from 32-bit or 16-bit floating point to
[38:35] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[38:40] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[38:45] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[38:50] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.
[38:55] Context length is a fundamental limitation of transformer models due to the quadratic scaling
[39:00] of attention with sequence length. Techniques like sliding window attention, sparse attention,
[39:05] and linear attention approximate the full attention mechanism at lower computational cost.
[39:10] Flash attention is a hardware-aware implementation that significantly reduces memory usage
[39:15] and speeds up attention computation by tiling the operation to fit in GPU on-chip memory.
[39:20] Retrieval augmented generation combines a language model with a retrieval system. At inference
[39:25] time, relevant documents are retrieved from a large corpus and included in the context window
[39:30] alongside the user query. This allows models to access up-to-date information without retraining
[39:35] and reduces hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved evidence.
[39:40] Evaluation of language models is challenging because the space of possible outputs is vast and
[39:45] quality is often subjective. Automatic benchmarks like MMLU, HellaSwag, and HumanEval measure
[39:50] specific capabilities. Human evaluation through preference comparisons or ratings provides a
[39:55] more holistic assessment. The ELO rating system borrowed from chess has been adapted to rank
[40:00] models based on human pairwise preferences across diverse tasks.
[40:05] Fine-tuning on domain-specific data is an effective way to improve model performance on
[40:10] specialized tasks. Parameter efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA and prefix tuning reduce
[40:15] the cost of adaptation by only training a small number of additional parameters. LoRA adds
[40:20] low-rank decomposition matrices to weight matrices and only trains these additions, leaving
[40:25] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.
[40:30] Welcome to this comprehensive deep dive into transformer architectures and large language models.

[58:50] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[58:55] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[59:00] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[59:05] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.
[59:10] Context length is a fundamental limitation of transformer models due to the quadratic scaling
[59:15] of attention with sequence length. Techniques like sliding window attention, sparse attention,
[59:20] and linear attention approximate the full attention mechanism at lower computational cost.
[59:25] Flash attention is a hardware-aware implementation that significantly reduces memory usage
[59:30] and speeds up attention computation by tiling the operation to fit in GPU on-chip memory.
[59:35] Retrieval augmented generation combines a language model with a retrieval system. At inference
[59:40] time, relevant documents are retrieved from a large corpus and included in the context window
[59:45] alongside the user query. This allows models to access up-to-date information without retraining
[59:50] and reduces hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved evidence.
[59:55] Evaluation of language models is challenging because the space of possible outputs is vast and
[01:00:00] quality is often subjective. Automatic benchmarks like MMLU, HellaSwag, and HumanEval measure
[01:00:05] specific capabilities. Human evaluation through preference comparisons or ratings provides a
[01:00:10] more holistic assessment. The ELO rating system borrowed from chess has been adapted to rank
[01:00:15] models based on human pairwise preferences across diverse tasks.
[01:00:20] Fine-tuning on domain-specific data is an effective way to improve model performance on
[01:00:25] specialized tasks. Parameter efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA and prefix tuning reduce
[01:00:30] the cost of adaptation by only training a small number of additional parameters. LoRA adds
[01:00:35] low-rank decomposition matrices to weight matrices and only trains these additions, leaving
[01:00:40] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.
[01:00:45] Welcome to this comprehensive deep dive into transformer architectures and large language models.
[01:00:50] In this lecture we will cover attention mechanisms, positional encodings, multi-head attention,
[01:00:55] feed-forward sublayers, layer normalization, residual connections, and the training dynamics
[01:01:00] that make these models so powerful. We will also discuss practical considerations like tokenization,
[01:01:05] vocabulary size, context length limitations, and inference optimization techniques such as
[01:01:10] key-value caching and speculative decoding. By the end of this session you will have a thorough
[01:01:15] understanding of how modern language models work under the hood and how to apply them effectively.
[01:01:20] The attention mechanism is the core innovation of the transformer. Unlike recurrent networks
[01:01:25] that process tokens sequentially, attention allows every token to directly attend to every other
[01:01:30] token in the sequence. The attention score between two tokens is computed as the dot product of
[01:01:35] their query and key vectors, scaled by the square root of the dimension, then passed through
[01:01:40] a softmax to produce a probability distribution. This distribution is used to compute a weighted
[01:01:45] sum of value vectors. The result is a context-aware representation for each token that captures
[01:01:50] relationships across the entire sequence regardless of distance.
[01:01:55] Multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel with different learned projections.
[01:02:00] Each head can specialize in different types of relationships. Some heads learn syntactic dependencies,
[01:02:05] others learn semantic associations, and others capture positional patterns. The outputs of all heads
[01:02:10] are concatenated and projected back to the model dimension. This gives the model much richer
[01:02:15] representational capacity than a single attention operation could provide.
[01:02:20] Positional encodings are added to token embeddings to inject information about position. The
[01:02:25] original transformer used sinusoidal encodings with different frequencies for each dimension.
[01:02:30] Later work explored learned positional embeddings, relative position encodings, and rotary
[01:02:35] position embeddings. Rotary encodings have become the dominant approach in modern models
[01:02:40] because they generalize well to sequence lengths beyond those seen during training.
[01:02:45] Layer normalization stabilizes training by normalizing the activations within each layer.
[01:02:50] Pre-norm architectures, where layer norm is applied before the attention and feed-forward
[01:02:55] sublayers rather than after, have been found to train more stably and are used in most modern
[01:03:00] large language models. Residual connections allow gradients to flow directly through the
[01:03:05] network during backpropagation, enabling training of very deep models.
[01:03:10] The feed-forward sublayer consists of two linear transformations with a non-linearity in between.
[01:03:15] The inner dimension is typically four times the model dimension. Modern models use the SwiGLU
[01:03:20] activation function, which is a variant of the gated linear unit, instead of the original ReLU.
[01:03:25] This change was found to improve performance across a wide range of tasks.
[01:03:30] Tokenization converts raw text into a sequence of integer indices that the model can process.
[01:03:35] Byte pair encoding is the most common tokenization algorithm. It starts with individual characters
[01:03:40] and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build a vocabulary of subword units.
[01:03:45] This balances vocabulary coverage with sequence length. Typical modern models use vocabularies
[01:03:50] of around thirty thousand to one hundred thousand tokens.
[01:03:55] Training large language models requires enormous computational resources. Models with billions
[01:04:00] of parameters are trained on trillions of tokens using hundreds or thousands of GPUs. Mixed
[01:04:05] precision training uses 16-bit floating point for most computations and 32-bit for weight updates
[01:04:10] to reduce memory usage and increase throughput. Gradient checkpointing trades compute for
[01:04:15] memory by recomputing activations during the backward pass instead of storing them.
[01:04:20] The training objective for language models is next-token prediction. The model is trained to
[01:04:25] maximize the log probability of the next token given all preceding tokens. This deceptively
[01:04:30] simple objective, when applied at massive scale, leads to models that acquire broad knowledge
[01:04:35] and surprising capabilities including reasoning, code generation, and instruction following.
[01:04:40] Instruction tuning is a fine-tuning procedure that teaches models to follow user instructions.
[01:04:45] A dataset of instruction-response pairs is collected and the model is fine-tuned on these examples.
[01:04:50] Reinforcement learning from human feedback further aligns the model by training a reward model
[01:04:55] on human preference data and using it to guide the policy model toward outputs that humans rate
[01:05:00] as more helpful, harmless, and honest.
[01:05:05] Inference optimization is critical for deploying large models efficiently. Key-value caching
[01:05:10] stores the key and value tensors from the attention mechanism so they do not need to be
[01:05:15] recomputed for tokens that have already been processed. Speculative decoding uses a small
[01:05:20] draft model to generate multiple candidate tokens and then verifies them in parallel with the
[01:05:25] large model, achieving significant speedups for autoregressive generation.
[01:05:30] Quantization reduces the precision of model weights from 32-bit or 16-bit floating point to
[01:05:35] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[01:05:40] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[01:05:45] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[01:05:50] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.
[01:05:55] Context length is a fundamental limitation of transformer models due to the quadratic scaling
[01:06:00] of attention with sequence length. Techniques like sliding window attention, sparse attention,
[01:06:05] and linear attention approximate the full attention mechanism at lower computational cost.
[01:06:10] Flash attention is a hardware-aware implementation that significantly reduces memory usage
[01:06:15] and speeds up attention computation by tiling the operation to fit in GPU on-chip memory.
[01:06:20] Retrieval augmented generation combines a language model with a retrieval system. At inference
[01:06:25] time, relevant documents are retrieved from a large corpus and included in the context window
[01:06:30] alongside the user query. This allows models to access up-to-date information without retraining
[01:06:35] and reduces hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved evidence.
[01:06:40] Evaluation of language models is challenging because the space of possible outputs is vast and
[01:06:45] quality is often subjective. Automatic benchmarks like MMLU, HellaSwag, and HumanEval measure
[01:06:50] specific capabilities. Human evaluation through preference comparisons or ratings provides a
[01:06:55] more holistic assessment. The ELO rating system borrowed from chess has been adapted to rank
[01:07:00] models based on human pairwise preferences across diverse tasks.
[01:07:05] Fine-tuning on domain-specific data is an effective way to improve model performance on
[01:07:10] specialized tasks. Parameter efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA and prefix tuning reduce
[01:07:15] the cost of adaptation by only training a small number of additional parameters. LoRA adds
[01:07:20] low-rank decomposition matrices to weight matrices and only trains these additions, leaving
[01:07:25] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.
[01:07:30] Welcome to this comprehensive deep dive into transformer architectures and large language models.
[01:07:35] In this lecture we will cover attention mechanisms, positional encodings, multi-head attention,
[01:07:40] feed-forward sublayers, layer normalization, residual connections, and the training dynamics

[01:07:50] vocabulary size, context length limitations, and inference optimization techniques such as
[01:07:55] key-value caching and speculative decoding. By the end of this session you will have a thorough
[01:08:00] understanding of how modern language models work under the hood and how to apply them effectively.

[01:09:50] network during backpropagation, enabling training of very deep models.

[01:10:00] The inner dimension is typically four times the model dimension. Modern models use the SwiGLU
[01:10:05] activation function, which is a variant of the gated linear unit, instead of the original ReLU.
[01:10:10] This change was found to improve performance across a wide range of tasks.

[01:10:25] and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build a vocabulary of subword units.

[01:10:35] of around thirty thousand to one hundred thousand tokens.

[01:14:05] low-rank decomposition matrices to weight matrices and only trains these additions, leaving
[01:14:10] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.

[01:16:55] This change was found to improve performance across a wide range of tasks.

[01:17:20] of around thirty thousand to one hundred thousand tokens.

[01:19:05] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[01:19:10] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization

[01:20:00] alongside the user query. This allows models to access up-to-date information without retraining
[01:20:05] and reduces hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved evidence.

[01:20:55] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.

[01:25:30] recomputed for tokens that have already been processed. Speculative decoding uses a small
[01:25:35] draft model to generate multiple candidate tokens and then verifies them in parallel with the
[01:25:40] large model, achieving significant speedups for autoregressive generation.

[01:25:50] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[01:25:55] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[01:26:00] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[01:26:05] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.
[01:26:10] Context length is a fundamental limitation of transformer models due to the quadratic scaling
[01:26:15] of attention with sequence length. Techniques like sliding window attention, sparse attention,
[01:26:20] and linear attention approximate the full attention mechanism at lower computational cost.
[01:26:25] Flash attention is a hardware-aware implementation that significantly reduces memory usage
[01:26:30] and speeds up attention computation by tiling the operation to fit in GPU on-chip memory.
[01:26:35] Retrieval augmented generation combines a language model with a retrieval system. At inference
[01:26:40] time, relevant documents are retrieved from a large corpus and included in the context window
[01:26:45] alongside the user query. This allows models to access up-to-date information without retraining
[01:26:50] and reduces hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved evidence.
[01:26:55] Evaluation of language models is challenging because the space of possible outputs is vast and
[01:27:00] quality is often subjective. Automatic benchmarks like MMLU, HellaSwag, and HumanEval measure
[01:27:05] specific capabilities. Human evaluation through preference comparisons or ratings provides a
[01:27:10] more holistic assessment. The ELO rating system borrowed from chess has been adapted to rank
[01:27:15] models based on human pairwise preferences across diverse tasks.
[01:27:20] Fine-tuning on domain-specific data is an effective way to improve model performance on
[01:27:25] specialized tasks. Parameter efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA and prefix tuning reduce
[01:27:30] the cost of adaptation by only training a small number of additional parameters. LoRA adds
[01:27:35] low-rank decomposition matrices to weight matrices and only trains these additions, leaving
[01:27:40] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.
[01:27:45] Welcome to this comprehensive deep dive into transformer architectures and large language models.
[01:27:50] In this lecture we will cover attention mechanisms, positional encodings, multi-head attention,
[01:27:55] feed-forward sublayers, layer normalization, residual connections, and the training dynamics
[01:28:00] that make these models so powerful. We will also discuss practical considerations like tokenization,
[01:28:05] vocabulary size, context length limitations, and inference optimization techniques such as
[01:28:10] key-value caching and speculative decoding. By the end of this session you will have a thorough
[01:28:15] understanding of how modern language models work under the hood and how to apply them effectively.
[01:28:20] The attention mechanism is the core innovation of the transformer. Unlike recurrent networks
[01:28:25] that process tokens sequentially, attention allows every token to directly attend to every other
[01:28:30] token in the sequence. The attention score between two tokens is computed as the dot product of
[01:28:35] their query and key vectors, scaled by the square root of the dimension, then passed through
[01:28:40] a softmax to produce a probability distribution. This distribution is used to compute a weighted
[01:28:45] sum of value vectors. The result is a context-aware representation for each token that captures
[01:28:50] relationships across the entire sequence regardless of distance.
[01:28:55] Multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel with different learned projections.
[01:29:00] Each head can specialize in different types of relationships. Some heads learn syntactic dependencies,
[01:29:05] others learn semantic associations, and others capture positional patterns. The outputs of all heads
[01:29:10] are concatenated and projected back to the model dimension. This gives the model much richer
[01:29:15] representational capacity than a single attention operation could provide.
[01:29:20] Positional encodings are added to token embeddings to inject information about position. The
[01:29:25] original transformer used sinusoidal encodings with different frequencies for each dimension.
[01:29:30] Later work explored learned positional embeddings, relative position encodings, and rotary
[01:29:35] position embeddings. Rotary encodings have become the dominant approach in modern models
[01:29:40] because they generalize well to sequence lengths beyond those seen during training.
[01:29:45] Layer normalization stabilizes training by normalizing the activations within each layer.
[01:29:50] Pre-norm architectures, where layer norm is applied before the attention and feed-forward
[01:29:55] sublayers rather than after, have been found to train more stably and are used in most modern
[01:30:00] large language models. Residual connections allow gradients to flow directly through the
[01:30:05] network during backpropagation, enabling training of very deep models.
[01:30:10] The feed-forward sublayer consists of two linear transformations with a non-linearity in between.
[01:30:15] The inner dimension is typically four times the model dimension. Modern models use the SwiGLU
[01:30:20] activation function, which is a variant of the gated linear unit, instead of the original ReLU.
[01:30:25] This change was found to improve performance across a wide range of tasks.
[01:30:30] Tokenization converts raw text into a sequence of integer indices that the model can process.
[01:30:35] Byte pair encoding is the most common tokenization algorithm. It starts with individual characters
[01:30:40] and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build a vocabulary of subword units.
[01:30:45] This balances vocabulary coverage with sequence length. Typical modern models use vocabularies
[01:30:50] of around thirty thousand to one hundred thousand tokens.
[01:30:55] Training large language models requires enormous computational resources. Models with billions
[01:31:00] of parameters are trained on trillions of tokens using hundreds or thousands of GPUs. Mixed
[01:31:05] precision training uses 16-bit floating point for most computations and 32-bit for weight updates
[01:31:10] to reduce memory usage and increase throughput. Gradient checkpointing trades compute for
[01:31:15] memory by recomputing activations during the backward pass instead of storing them.
[01:31:20] The training objective for language models is next-token prediction. The model is trained to
[01:31:25] maximize the log probability of the next token given all preceding tokens. This deceptively
[01:31:30] simple objective, when applied at massive scale, leads to models that acquire broad knowledge
[01:31:35] and surprising capabilities including reasoning, code generation, and instruction following.
[01:31:40] Instruction tuning is a fine-tuning procedure that teaches models to follow user instructions.
[01:31:45] A dataset of instruction-response pairs is collected and the model is fine-tuned on these examples.
[01:31:50] Reinforcement learning from human feedback further aligns the model by training a reward model
[01:31:55] on human preference data and using it to guide the policy model toward outputs that humans rate
[01:32:00] as more helpful, harmless, and honest.
[01:32:05] Inference optimization is critical for deploying large models efficiently. Key-value caching
[01:32:10] stores the key and value tensors from the attention mechanism so they do not need to be
[01:32:15] recomputed for tokens that have already been processed. Speculative decoding uses a small
[01:32:20] draft model to generate multiple candidate tokens and then verifies them in parallel with the
[01:32:25] large model, achieving significant speedups for autoregressive generation.
[01:32:30] Quantization reduces the precision of model weights from 32-bit or 16-bit floating point to
[01:32:35] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[01:32:40] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[01:32:45] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[01:32:50] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.
[01:32:55] Context length is a fundamental limitation of transformer models due to the quadratic scaling
[01:33:00] of attention with sequence length. Techniques like sliding window attention, sparse attention,
[01:33:05] and linear attention approximate the full attention mechanism at lower computational cost.
[01:33:10] Flash attention is a hardware-aware implementation that significantly reduces memory usage
[01:33:15] and speeds up attention computation by tiling the operation to fit in GPU on-chip memory.
[01:33:20] Retrieval augmented generation combines a language model with a retrieval system. At inference
[01:33:25] time, relevant documents are retrieved from a large corpus and included in the context window
[01:33:30] alongside the user query. This allows models to access up-to-date information without retraining
[01:33:35] and reduces hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved evidence.
[01:33:40] Evaluation of language models is challenging because the space of possible outputs is vast and
[01:33:45] quality is often subjective. Automatic benchmarks like MMLU, HellaSwag, and HumanEval measure
[01:33:50] specific capabilities. Human evaluation through preference comparisons or ratings provides a
[01:33:55] more holistic assessment. The ELO rating system borrowed from chess has been adapted to rank
[01:34:00] models based on human pairwise preferences across diverse tasks.
[01:34:05] Fine-tuning on domain-specific data is an effective way to improve model performance on
[01:34:10] specialized tasks. Parameter efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA and prefix tuning reduce
[01:34:15] the cost of adaptation by only training a small number of additional parameters. LoRA adds
[01:34:20] low-rank decomposition matrices to weight matrices and only trains these additions, leaving
[01:34:25] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.
[01:34:30] Welcome to this comprehensive deep dive into transformer architectures and large language models.
[01:34:35] In this lecture we will cover attention mechanisms, positional encodings, multi-head attention,
[01:34:40] feed-forward sublayers, layer normalization, residual connections, and the training dynamics
[01:34:45] that make these models so powerful. We will also discuss practical considerations like tokenization,
[01:34:50] vocabulary size, context length limitations, and inference optimization techniques such as
[01:34:55] key-value caching and speculative decoding. By the end of this session you will have a thorough
[01:35:00] understanding of how modern language models work under the hood and how to apply them effectively.

[01:35:10] that process tokens sequentially, attention allows every token to directly attend to every other
[01:35:15] token in the sequence. The attention score between two tokens is computed as the dot product of
[01:35:20] their query and key vectors, scaled by the square root of the dimension, then passed through
[01:35:25] a softmax to produce a probability distribution. This distribution is used to compute a weighted
[01:35:30] sum of value vectors. The result is a context-aware representation for each token that captures
[01:35:35] relationships across the entire sequence regardless of distance.

[01:35:50] others learn semantic associations, and others capture positional patterns. The outputs of all heads
[01:35:55] are concatenated and projected back to the model dimension. This gives the model much richer
[01:36:00] representational capacity than a single attention operation could provide.

[01:36:10] original transformer used sinusoidal encodings with different frequencies for each dimension.

[01:36:20] position embeddings. Rotary encodings have become the dominant approach in modern models
[01:36:25] because they generalize well to sequence lengths beyond those seen during training.

[01:36:40] sublayers rather than after, have been found to train more stably and are used in most modern
[01:36:45] large language models. Residual connections allow gradients to flow directly through the
[01:36:50] network during backpropagation, enabling training of very deep models.

[01:37:00] The inner dimension is typically four times the model dimension. Modern models use the SwiGLU
[01:37:05] activation function, which is a variant of the gated linear unit, instead of the original ReLU.
[01:37:10] This change was found to improve performance across a wide range of tasks.
[01:37:15] Tokenization converts raw text into a sequence of integer indices that the model can process.
[01:37:20] Byte pair encoding is the most common tokenization algorithm. It starts with individual characters
[01:37:25] and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build a vocabulary of subword units.
[01:37:30] This balances vocabulary coverage with sequence length. Typical modern models use vocabularies
[01:37:35] of around thirty thousand to one hundred thousand tokens.

[01:37:45] of parameters are trained on trillions of tokens using hundreds or thousands of GPUs. Mixed

[01:38:15] simple objective, when applied at massive scale, leads to models that acquire broad knowledge
[01:38:20] and surprising capabilities including reasoning, code generation, and instruction following.

[01:38:40] on human preference data and using it to guide the policy model toward outputs that humans rate
[01:38:45] as more helpful, harmless, and honest.

[01:39:20] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[01:39:25] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[01:39:30] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[01:39:35] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.

[01:39:50] and linear attention approximate the full attention mechanism at lower computational cost.

[01:40:15] alongside the user query. This allows models to access up-to-date information without retraining
[01:40:20] and reduces hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved evidence.

[01:41:05] low-rank decomposition matrices to weight matrices and only trains these additions, leaving
[01:41:10] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.

[01:43:55] This change was found to improve performance across a wide range of tasks.

[01:44:20] of around thirty thousand to one hundred thousand tokens.

[01:46:05] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[01:46:10] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[01:46:15] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[01:46:20] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.

[01:52:40] large model, achieving significant speedups for autoregressive generation.

[01:52:50] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[01:52:55] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[01:53:00] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[01:53:05] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.
[01:53:10] Context length is a fundamental limitation of transformer models due to the quadratic scaling
[01:53:15] of attention with sequence length. Techniques like sliding window attention, sparse attention,
[01:53:20] and linear attention approximate the full attention mechanism at lower computational cost.
[01:53:25] Flash attention is a hardware-aware implementation that significantly reduces memory usage
[01:53:30] and speeds up attention computation by tiling the operation to fit in GPU on-chip memory.
[01:53:35] Retrieval augmented generation combines a language model with a retrieval system. At inference
[01:53:40] time, relevant documents are retrieved from a large corpus and included in the context window
[01:53:45] alongside the user query. This allows models to access up-to-date information without retraining
[01:53:50] and reduces hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved evidence.
[01:53:55] Evaluation of language models is challenging because the space of possible outputs is vast and
[01:54:00] quality is often subjective. Automatic benchmarks like MMLU, HellaSwag, and HumanEval measure
[01:54:05] specific capabilities. Human evaluation through preference comparisons or ratings provides a
[01:54:10] more holistic assessment. The ELO rating system borrowed from chess has been adapted to rank
[01:54:15] models based on human pairwise preferences across diverse tasks.
[01:54:20] Fine-tuning on domain-specific data is an effective way to improve model performance on
[01:54:25] specialized tasks. Parameter efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA and prefix tuning reduce
[01:54:30] the cost of adaptation by only training a small number of additional parameters. LoRA adds
[01:54:35] low-rank decomposition matrices to weight matrices and only trains these additions, leaving
[01:54:40] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.
[01:54:45] Welcome to this comprehensive deep dive into transformer architectures and large language models.
[01:54:50] In this lecture we will cover attention mechanisms, positional encodings, multi-head attention,
[01:54:55] feed-forward sublayers, layer normalization, residual connections, and the training dynamics
[01:55:00] that make these models so powerful. We will also discuss practical considerations like tokenization,
[01:55:05] vocabulary size, context length limitations, and inference optimization techniques such as
[01:55:10] key-value caching and speculative decoding. By the end of this session you will have a thorough
[01:55:15] understanding of how modern language models work under the hood and how to apply them effectively.
[01:55:20] The attention mechanism is the core innovation of the transformer. Unlike recurrent networks
[01:55:25] that process tokens sequentially, attention allows every token to directly attend to every other
[01:55:30] token in the sequence. The attention score between two tokens is computed as the dot product of
[01:55:35] their query and key vectors, scaled by the square root of the dimension, then passed through
[01:55:40] a softmax to produce a probability distribution. This distribution is used to compute a weighted
[01:55:45] sum of value vectors. The result is a context-aware representation for each token that captures
[01:55:50] relationships across the entire sequence regardless of distance.
[01:55:55] Multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel with different learned projections.
[01:56:00] Each head can specialize in different types of relationships. Some heads learn syntactic dependencies,
[01:56:05] others learn semantic associations, and others capture positional patterns. The outputs of all heads
[01:56:10] are concatenated and projected back to the model dimension. This gives the model much richer
[01:56:15] representational capacity than a single attention operation could provide.
[01:56:20] Positional encodings are added to token embeddings to inject information about position. The
[01:56:25] original transformer used sinusoidal encodings with different frequencies for each dimension.
[01:56:30] Later work explored learned positional embeddings, relative position encodings, and rotary
[01:56:35] position embeddings. Rotary encodings have become the dominant approach in modern models
[01:56:40] because they generalize well to sequence lengths beyond those seen during training.
[01:56:45] Layer normalization stabilizes training by normalizing the activations within each layer.
[01:56:50] Pre-norm architectures, where layer norm is applied before the attention and feed-forward
[01:56:55] sublayers rather than after, have been found to train more stably and are used in most modern
[01:57:00] large language models. Residual connections allow gradients to flow directly through the
[01:57:05] network during backpropagation, enabling training of very deep models.
[01:57:10] The feed-forward sublayer consists of two linear transformations with a non-linearity in between.
[01:57:15] The inner dimension is typically four times the model dimension. Modern models use the SwiGLU
[01:57:20] activation function, which is a variant of the gated linear unit, instead of the original ReLU.
[01:57:25] This change was found to improve performance across a wide range of tasks.
[01:57:30] Tokenization converts raw text into a sequence of integer indices that the model can process.
[01:57:35] Byte pair encoding is the most common tokenization algorithm. It starts with individual characters
[01:57:40] and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build a vocabulary of subword units.
[01:57:45] This balances vocabulary coverage with sequence length. Typical modern models use vocabularies
[01:57:50] of around thirty thousand to one hundred thousand tokens.
[01:57:55] Training large language models requires enormous computational resources. Models with billions
[01:58:00] of parameters are trained on trillions of tokens using hundreds or thousands of GPUs. Mixed
[01:58:05] precision training uses 16-bit floating point for most computations and 32-bit for weight updates
[01:58:10] to reduce memory usage and increase throughput. Gradient checkpointing trades compute for
[01:58:15] memory by recomputing activations during the backward pass instead of storing them.
[01:58:20] The training objective for language models is next-token prediction. The model is trained to
[01:58:25] maximize the log probability of the next token given all preceding tokens. This deceptively
[01:58:30] simple objective, when applied at massive scale, leads to models that acquire broad knowledge
[01:58:35] and surprising capabilities including reasoning, code generation, and instruction following.
[01:58:40] Instruction tuning is a fine-tuning procedure that teaches models to follow user instructions.
[01:58:45] A dataset of instruction-response pairs is collected and the model is fine-tuned on these examples.
[01:58:50] Reinforcement learning from human feedback further aligns the model by training a reward model
[01:58:55] on human preference data and using it to guide the policy model toward outputs that humans rate
[01:59:00] as more helpful, harmless, and honest.
[01:59:05] Inference optimization is critical for deploying large models efficiently. Key-value caching
[01:59:10] stores the key and value tensors from the attention mechanism so they do not need to be
[01:59:15] recomputed for tokens that have already been processed. Speculative decoding uses a small
[01:59:20] draft model to generate multiple candidate tokens and then verifies them in parallel with the
[01:59:25] large model, achieving significant speedups for autoregressive generation.
[01:59:30] Quantization reduces the precision of model weights from 32-bit or 16-bit floating point to
[01:59:35] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[01:59:40] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[01:59:45] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[01:59:50] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.
[01:59:55] Context length is a fundamental limitation of transformer models due to the quadratic scaling
[02:00:00] of attention with sequence length. Techniques like sliding window attention, sparse attention,
[02:00:05] and linear attention approximate the full attention mechanism at lower computational cost.
[02:00:10] Flash attention is a hardware-aware implementation that significantly reduces memory usage
[02:00:15] and speeds up attention computation by tiling the operation to fit in GPU on-chip memory.
[02:00:20] Retrieval augmented generation combines a language model with a retrieval system. At inference
[02:00:25] time, relevant documents are retrieved from a large corpus and included in the context window
[02:00:30] alongside the user query. This allows models to access up-to-date information without retraining
[02:00:35] and reduces hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved evidence.
[02:00:40] Evaluation of language models is challenging because the space of possible outputs is vast and
[02:00:45] quality is often subjective. Automatic benchmarks like MMLU, HellaSwag, and HumanEval measure
[02:00:50] specific capabilities. Human evaluation through preference comparisons or ratings provides a
[02:00:55] more holistic assessment. The ELO rating system borrowed from chess has been adapted to rank
[02:01:00] models based on human pairwise preferences across diverse tasks.
[02:01:05] Fine-tuning on domain-specific data is an effective way to improve model performance on
[02:01:10] specialized tasks. Parameter efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA and prefix tuning reduce
[02:01:15] the cost of adaptation by only training a small number of additional parameters. LoRA adds
[02:01:20] low-rank decomposition matrices to weight matrices and only trains these additions, leaving
[02:01:25] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.
[02:01:30] Welcome to this comprehensive deep dive into transformer architectures and large language models.
[02:01:35] In this lecture we will cover attention mechanisms, positional encodings, multi-head attention,
[02:01:40] feed-forward sublayers, layer normalization, residual connections, and the training dynamics
[02:01:45] that make these models so powerful. We will also discuss practical considerations like tokenization,
[02:01:50] vocabulary size, context length limitations, and inference optimization techniques such as
[02:01:55] key-value caching and speculative decoding. By the end of this session you will have a thorough
[02:02:00] understanding of how modern language models work under the hood and how to apply them effectively.
[02:02:05] The attention mechanism is the core innovation of the transformer. Unlike recurrent networks
[02:02:10] that process tokens sequentially, attention allows every token to directly attend to every other
[02:02:15] token in the sequence. The attention score between two tokens is computed as the dot product of
[02:02:20] their query and key vectors, scaled by the square root of the dimension, then passed through
[02:02:25] a softmax to produce a probability distribution. This distribution is used to compute a weighted
[02:02:30] sum of value vectors. The result is a context-aware representation for each token that captures
[02:02:35] relationships across the entire sequence regardless of distance.
[02:02:40] Multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel with different learned projections.
[02:02:45] Each head can specialize in different types of relationships. Some heads learn syntactic dependencies,
[02:02:50] others learn semantic associations, and others capture positional patterns. The outputs of all heads
[02:02:55] are concatenated and projected back to the model dimension. This gives the model much richer
[02:03:00] representational capacity than a single attention operation could provide.

[02:03:10] original transformer used sinusoidal encodings with different frequencies for each dimension.
[02:03:15] Later work explored learned positional embeddings, relative position encodings, and rotary
[02:03:20] position embeddings. Rotary encodings have become the dominant approach in modern models
[02:03:25] because they generalize well to sequence lengths beyond those seen during training.

[02:03:35] Pre-norm architectures, where layer norm is applied before the attention and feed-forward
[02:03:40] sublayers rather than after, have been found to train more stably and are used in most modern
[02:03:45] large language models. Residual connections allow gradients to flow directly through the
[02:03:50] network during backpropagation, enabling training of very deep models.
[02:03:55] The feed-forward sublayer consists of two linear transformations with a non-linearity in between.
[02:04:00] The inner dimension is typically four times the model dimension. Modern models use the SwiGLU
[02:04:05] activation function, which is a variant of the gated linear unit, instead of the original ReLU.
[02:04:10] This change was found to improve performance across a wide range of tasks.
[02:04:15] Tokenization converts raw text into a sequence of integer indices that the model can process.
[02:04:20] Byte pair encoding is the most common tokenization algorithm. It starts with individual characters
[02:04:25] and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build a vocabulary of subword units.
[02:04:30] This balances vocabulary coverage with sequence length. Typical modern models use vocabularies
[02:04:35] of around thirty thousand to one hundred thousand tokens.

[02:04:45] of parameters are trained on trillions of tokens using hundreds or thousands of GPUs. Mixed
[02:04:50] precision training uses 16-bit floating point for most computations and 32-bit for weight updates
[02:04:55] to reduce memory usage and increase throughput. Gradient checkpointing trades compute for
[02:05:00] memory by recomputing activations during the backward pass instead of storing them.

[02:05:10] maximize the log probability of the next token given all preceding tokens. This deceptively
[02:05:15] simple objective, when applied at massive scale, leads to models that acquire broad knowledge
[02:05:20] and surprising capabilities including reasoning, code generation, and instruction following.

[02:05:30] A dataset of instruction-response pairs is collected and the model is fine-tuned on these examples.

[02:05:40] on human preference data and using it to guide the policy model toward outputs that humans rate
[02:05:45] as more helpful, harmless, and honest.

[02:05:55] stores the key and value tensors from the attention mechanism so they do not need to be
[02:06:00] recomputed for tokens that have already been processed. Speculative decoding uses a small
[02:06:05] draft model to generate multiple candidate tokens and then verifies them in parallel with the
[02:06:10] large model, achieving significant speedups for autoregressive generation.

[02:06:20] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[02:06:25] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[02:06:30] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[02:06:35] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.

[02:06:45] of attention with sequence length. Techniques like sliding window attention, sparse attention,
[02:06:50] and linear attention approximate the full attention mechanism at lower computational cost.

[02:08:05] low-rank decomposition matrices to weight matrices and only trains these additions, leaving
[02:08:10] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.

[02:10:10] because they generalize well to sequence lengths beyond those seen during training.

[02:10:50] activation function, which is a variant of the gated linear unit, instead of the original ReLU.
[02:10:55] This change was found to improve performance across a wide range of tasks.

[02:11:10] and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build a vocabulary of subword units.

[02:11:20] of around thirty thousand to one hundred thousand tokens.

[02:13:05] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[02:13:10] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[02:13:15] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most

[02:14:55] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.

[02:19:15] as more helpful, harmless, and honest.

[02:19:35] draft model to generate multiple candidate tokens and then verifies them in parallel with the
[02:19:40] large model, achieving significant speedups for autoregressive generation.
