[02:19:50] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[02:19:55] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[02:20:00] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[02:20:05] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.
[02:20:10] Context length is a fundamental limitation of transformer models due to the quadratic scaling
[02:20:15] of attention with sequence length. Techniques like sliding window attention, sparse attention,
[02:20:20] and linear attention approximate the full attention mechanism at lower computational cost.
[02:20:25] Flash attention is a hardware-aware implementation that significantly reduces memory usage
[02:20:30] and speeds up attention computation by tiling the operation to fit in GPU on-chip memory.
[02:20:35] Retrieval augmented generation combines a language model with a retrieval system. At inference
[02:20:40] time, relevant documents are retrieved from a large corpus and included in the context window
[02:20:45] alongside the user query. This allows models to access up-to-date information without retraining
[02:20:50] and reduces hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved evidence.
[02:20:55] Evaluation of language models is challenging because the space of possible outputs is vast and
[02:21:00] quality is often subjective. Automatic benchmarks like MMLU, HellaSwag, and HumanEval measure
[02:21:05] specific capabilities. Human evaluation through preference comparisons or ratings provides a
[02:21:10] more holistic assessment. The ELO rating system borrowed from chess has been adapted to rank
[02:21:15] models based on human pairwise preferences across diverse tasks.
[02:21:20] Fine-tuning on domain-specific data is an effective way to improve model performance on
[02:21:25] specialized tasks. Parameter efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA and prefix tuning reduce
[02:21:30] the cost of adaptation by only training a small number of additional parameters. LoRA adds
[02:21:35] low-rank decomposition matrices to weight matrices and only trains these additions, leaving
[02:21:40] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.
[02:21:45] Welcome to this comprehensive deep dive into transformer architectures and large language models.
[02:21:50] In this lecture we will cover attention mechanisms, positional encodings, multi-head attention,
[02:21:55] feed-forward sublayers, layer normalization, residual connections, and the training dynamics
[02:22:00] that make these models so powerful. We will also discuss practical considerations like tokenization,
[02:22:05] vocabulary size, context length limitations, and inference optimization techniques such as
[02:22:10] key-value caching and speculative decoding. By the end of this session you will have a thorough
[02:22:15] understanding of how modern language models work under the hood and how to apply them effectively.
[02:22:20] The attention mechanism is the core innovation of the transformer. Unlike recurrent networks
[02:22:25] that process tokens sequentially, attention allows every token to directly attend to every other
[02:22:30] token in the sequence. The attention score between two tokens is computed as the dot product of
[02:22:35] their query and key vectors, scaled by the square root of the dimension, then passed through
[02:22:40] a softmax to produce a probability distribution. This distribution is used to compute a weighted
[02:22:45] sum of value vectors. The result is a context-aware representation for each token that captures
[02:22:50] relationships across the entire sequence regardless of distance.
[02:22:55] Multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel with different learned projections.
[02:23:00] Each head can specialize in different types of relationships. Some heads learn syntactic dependencies,
[02:23:05] others learn semantic associations, and others capture positional patterns. The outputs of all heads
[02:23:10] are concatenated and projected back to the model dimension. This gives the model much richer
[02:23:15] representational capacity than a single attention operation could provide.
[02:23:20] Positional encodings are added to token embeddings to inject information about position. The
[02:23:25] original transformer used sinusoidal encodings with different frequencies for each dimension.
[02:23:30] Later work explored learned positional embeddings, relative position encodings, and rotary
[02:23:35] position embeddings. Rotary encodings have become the dominant approach in modern models
[02:23:40] because they generalize well to sequence lengths beyond those seen during training.
[02:23:45] Layer normalization stabilizes training by normalizing the activations within each layer.
[02:23:50] Pre-norm architectures, where layer norm is applied before the attention and feed-forward
[02:23:55] sublayers rather than after, have been found to train more stably and are used in most modern
[02:24:00] large language models. Residual connections allow gradients to flow directly through the
[02:24:05] network during backpropagation, enabling training of very deep models.
[02:24:10] The feed-forward sublayer consists of two linear transformations with a non-linearity in between.
[02:24:15] The inner dimension is typically four times the model dimension. Modern models use the SwiGLU
[02:24:20] activation function, which is a variant of the gated linear unit, instead of the original ReLU.
[02:24:25] This change was found to improve performance across a wide range of tasks.
[02:24:30] Tokenization converts raw text into a sequence of integer indices that the model can process.
[02:24:35] Byte pair encoding is the most common tokenization algorithm. It starts with individual characters
[02:24:40] and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build a vocabulary of subword units.
[02:24:45] This balances vocabulary coverage with sequence length. Typical modern models use vocabularies
[02:24:50] of around thirty thousand to one hundred thousand tokens.
[02:24:55] Training large language models requires enormous computational resources. Models with billions
[02:25:00] of parameters are trained on trillions of tokens using hundreds or thousands of GPUs. Mixed
[02:25:05] precision training uses 16-bit floating point for most computations and 32-bit for weight updates
[02:25:10] to reduce memory usage and increase throughput. Gradient checkpointing trades compute for
[02:25:15] memory by recomputing activations during the backward pass instead of storing them.
[02:25:20] The training objective for language models is next-token prediction. The model is trained to
[02:25:25] maximize the log probability of the next token given all preceding tokens. This deceptively
[02:25:30] simple objective, when applied at massive scale, leads to models that acquire broad knowledge
[02:25:35] and surprising capabilities including reasoning, code generation, and instruction following.
[02:25:40] Instruction tuning is a fine-tuning procedure that teaches models to follow user instructions.
[02:25:45] A dataset of instruction-response pairs is collected and the model is fine-tuned on these examples.
[02:25:50] Reinforcement learning from human feedback further aligns the model by training a reward model
[02:25:55] on human preference data and using it to guide the policy model toward outputs that humans rate
[02:26:00] as more helpful, harmless, and honest.
[02:26:05] Inference optimization is critical for deploying large models efficiently. Key-value caching
[02:26:10] stores the key and value tensors from the attention mechanism so they do not need to be
[02:26:15] recomputed for tokens that have already been processed. Speculative decoding uses a small
[02:26:20] draft model to generate multiple candidate tokens and then verifies them in parallel with the
[02:26:25] large model, achieving significant speedups for autoregressive generation.
[02:26:30] Quantization reduces the precision of model weights from 32-bit or 16-bit floating point to
[02:26:35] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[02:26:40] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[02:26:45] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[02:26:50] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.
[02:26:55] Context length is a fundamental limitation of transformer models due to the quadratic scaling
[02:27:00] of attention with sequence length. Techniques like sliding window attention, sparse attention,
[02:27:05] and linear attention approximate the full attention mechanism at lower computational cost.
[02:27:10] Flash attention is a hardware-aware implementation that significantly reduces memory usage
[02:27:15] and speeds up attention computation by tiling the operation to fit in GPU on-chip memory.
[02:27:20] Retrieval augmented generation combines a language model with a retrieval system. At inference
[02:27:25] time, relevant documents are retrieved from a large corpus and included in the context window
[02:27:30] alongside the user query. This allows models to access up-to-date information without retraining
[02:27:35] and reduces hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved evidence.
[02:27:40] Evaluation of language models is challenging because the space of possible outputs is vast and
[02:27:45] quality is often subjective. Automatic benchmarks like MMLU, HellaSwag, and HumanEval measure
[02:27:50] specific capabilities. Human evaluation through preference comparisons or ratings provides a
[02:27:55] more holistic assessment. The ELO rating system borrowed from chess has been adapted to rank
[02:28:00] models based on human pairwise preferences across diverse tasks.
[02:28:05] Fine-tuning on domain-specific data is an effective way to improve model performance on
[02:28:10] specialized tasks. Parameter efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA and prefix tuning reduce
[02:28:15] the cost of adaptation by only training a small number of additional parameters. LoRA adds
[02:28:20] low-rank decomposition matrices to weight matrices and only trains these additions, leaving
[02:28:25] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.
[02:28:30] Welcome to this comprehensive deep dive into transformer architectures and large language models.

[02:28:40] feed-forward sublayers, layer normalization, residual connections, and the training dynamics

[02:29:00] understanding of how modern language models work under the hood and how to apply them effectively.

[02:31:05] activation function, which is a variant of the gated linear unit, instead of the original ReLU.
[02:31:10] This change was found to improve performance across a wide range of tasks.

[02:31:25] and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build a vocabulary of subword units.
[02:31:30] This balances vocabulary coverage with sequence length. Typical modern models use vocabularies
[02:31:35] of around thirty thousand to one hundred thousand tokens.

[02:31:45] of parameters are trained on trillions of tokens using hundreds or thousands of GPUs. Mixed

[02:32:45] as more helpful, harmless, and honest.

[02:33:20] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[02:33:25] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[02:33:30] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[02:33:35] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.

[02:33:50] and linear attention approximate the full attention mechanism at lower computational cost.

[02:34:10] time, relevant documents are retrieved from a large corpus and included in the context window
[02:34:15] alongside the user query. This allows models to access up-to-date information without retraining
[02:34:20] and reduces hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved evidence.

[02:35:10] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.

[02:37:05] position embeddings. Rotary encodings have become the dominant approach in modern models

[02:37:50] activation function, which is a variant of the gated linear unit, instead of the original ReLU.
[02:37:55] This change was found to improve performance across a wide range of tasks.

[02:38:10] and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build a vocabulary of subword units.
[02:38:15] This balances vocabulary coverage with sequence length. Typical modern models use vocabularies
[02:38:20] of around thirty thousand to one hundred thousand tokens.

[02:38:40] to reduce memory usage and increase throughput. Gradient checkpointing trades compute for

[02:40:05] 8-bit integers or lower. This reduces memory footprint and can increase throughput on hardware
[02:40:10] that supports integer arithmetic. Techniques like GPTQ and AWQ perform weight quantization
[02:40:15] while preserving model quality. 4-bit quantization with careful calibration can retain most
[02:40:20] of the original model performance while cutting memory requirements by four times.

[02:40:30] of attention with sequence length. Techniques like sliding window attention, sparse attention,
[02:40:35] and linear attention approximate the full attention mechanism at lower computational cost.

[02:40:55] time, relevant documents are retrieved from a large corpus and included in the context window
[02:41:00] alongside the user query. This allows models to access up-to-date information without retraining
[02:41:05] and reduces hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved evidence.

[02:41:15] quality is often subjective. Automatic benchmarks like MMLU, HellaSwag, and HumanEval measure
[02:41:20] specific capabilities. Human evaluation through preference comparisons or ratings provides a
[02:41:25] more holistic assessment. The ELO rating system borrowed from chess has been adapted to rank

[02:41:55] the original weights frozen. This can match full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of the cost.
